title: Little Sister

jokes.text: Moira panicking about invisible lice/scabies/bed bugs in the bed while John repeatedly says 'What?'
jokes.setup: Something is in the bed and Moira can't see it
jokes.punchline: 'Something invisible like lice or scabies or bed bugs!' — escalating from a spider to invisible parasites, with Moira's aristocratic panic about burning her clothes
jokes.explanation: The comedy comes from Moira's immediate leap to invisible parasites — classic character comedy showing her hypochondria and melodrama. The repeated 'What?' from John grounds it nicely. Good character integration for Moira but the setup is fairly standard.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: There's something crawling up my leg!

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: What is it?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I can't see it. It's invisible like lice or scabies or bed bugs.

jokes.text: Moira: 'John, I can't afford to burn all my clothes.'
jokes.setup: Moira is panicking about possible lice or bed bugs
jokes.punchline: Her concern is not the infestation itself but the financial cost of burning her wardrobe — revealing both her vanity and their new financial reality
jokes.explanation: Tight, specific, and deeply character-rooted. Only Moira would respond to a possible lice scare by worrying about whether she can afford to burn her clothes. The line quietly acknowledges their financial ruin while staying in her voice. Excellent economy — does double duty in very few words.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We have bed bugs! I can feel them crawling all over me!

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: Moira, we don't have bed bugs.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: How can you be so sure?

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: Because bed bugs have standards.

jokes.text: Dee Dee: 'Do you really have lice? I'd hate to contract lice.'
jokes.setup: Dee Dee has just surprised her sister with an unannounced visit
jokes.punchline: Her first expressed concern upon seeing her long-lost sister is whether she'll catch lice
jokes.explanation: Quick and efficient character establishment for Dee Dee — she's self-absorbed, just like Moira. The line is funny because it mirrors Moira's own vanity back at her from an outside character. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We need to burn everything. All of it. Every. Single. Thing.

jokes.text: Dee Dee: 'Seaweed facials. I get one every week. But enough about me, I wanna talk about you guys.'
jokes.setup: John compliments Dee Dee on looking well
jokes.punchline: She immediately pivots from a vanity detail to claiming she wants to focus on others — but she's already proven she can't stop talking about herself
jokes.explanation: The quick pivot from 'seaweed facials' to 'enough about me' is a reliable but well-executed self-absorbed character gag. The irony is clear and clean.

dialogue.speaker: Dee Dee
dialogue.line: Guess who?

jokes.text: Dee Dee explains she wanted to visit 'when I heard about your life collapsing' but got a promotion that week and things got 'kind of crazy.'
jokes.setup: Dee Dee is explaining her absence during the Roses' financial ruin
jokes.punchline: She casually describes their total life collapse as an afterthought to her own good news, delivered with breezy unawareness
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy — Dee Dee is completely oblivious to how her excuse sounds. The phrase 'life collapsing' used so casually is the operative joke. Shows strong character specificity for Dee Dee.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh my God, is that Dee Dee? What are you doing here?

dialogue.speaker: Dee Dee
dialogue.line: I came to support you all through this difficult time.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, that's very thoughtful.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, how wonderful. Do you have any open sores? Any unusual discharge? Have you been in contact with anyone from the developing world?

dialogue.speaker: Dee Dee
dialogue.line: I... what? I'm fine, I just got off a plane.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Exactly. We need to quarantine you immediately.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Telephone is also amazing. You just push the buttons and say, Hi, it's me, your deadbeat sister.'
jokes.setup: Dee Dee says the internet kept her connected to the Roses
jokes.punchline: Moira sarcastically narrates a phone call Dee Dee never made, with theatrical precision and calling her a 'deadbeat sister'
jokes.explanation: Very Moira — she can't help the theatrical precision. 'You just push the buttons' is patronizingly instructive, and 'Hi, it's me, your deadbeat sister' is a sharp payoff. Good character voice and tight structure.

dialogue.speaker: Dee Dee
dialogue.line: I know I should have come by during your whole thing, but my promotion was really taking off and I just couldn't get away.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Our life was collapsing.

dialogue.speaker: Dee Dee
dialogue.line: Well, yes, but like I said, the promotion.

jokes.text: John: 'Kids! Come in and say hi to your Aunt Dee Dee. Better hurry before she disappears for another decade.'
jokes.setup: John is cheerfully calling the kids to meet their aunt
jokes.punchline: He casually drops the dig about her decade-long absence while maintaining a pleasant hosting tone
jokes.explanation: John gets a rare sardonic moment here. The joke works because it's delivered as if it's just practical advice. The contrast between the cheerful 'come say hi!' and the pointed 'before she disappears for another decade' is well-constructed.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I only knew you were alive through internet news of your downfall.

jokes.text: Dee Dee to the kids: 'Why did I think that you were dead?'
jokes.setup: Dee Dee is gushing over the kids she hasn't seen in years
jokes.punchline: She casually admits she thought they were dead — as if that were a reasonable assumption — delivered with warmth
jokes.explanation: This is a standout line. The admission is absurd — she thought her niece and nephew were dead and is only mildly correcting herself now — and is delivered as a sweet aside. The dark content wrapped in warm affect is quintessential Schitt's Creek comedy. Very quotable and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It would have been so easy to just call. See? Easy. Hello, Dee Dee, you deadbeat.

jokes.text: David to the kids about Dee Dee: 'I think you crashed one of my pool parties with some guy you were calling Uncle Julio.'
jokes.setup: The family is catching up on how long it's been since they've seen each other
jokes.punchline: David's memory of Dee Dee involves her crashing a party with a random man given the family title 'Uncle Julio'
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Uncle Julio' is the joke. It implies Dee Dee has a rotating cast of men the family is casually assigned to call uncle. Good world-building comedy with efficient delivery.

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: Where have you been? We've been worried sick!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, it's been far too long. We thought perhaps you'd forgotten us entirely.

jokes.text: Dee Dee gives the kids $100 cash and they react with overwhelming excitement
jokes.setup: Dee Dee is handing out cash gifts to the kids
jokes.punchline: The formerly ultra-wealthy Rose children are overjoyed by a $100 bill — a measure of how far they've fallen
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the reactions — kids who once had everything genuinely moved by $100. Solid character-based comedy but fairly predictable given the show's premise.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, hi! I haven't seen you in like... forever. I literally thought you were dead.

dialogue.speaker: Aunt
dialogue.line: I'm not dead, Alexis.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I know that now, obviously. But like, why did I think that? Did you fake your death or something?

jokes.text: Dee Dee: 'No. God, no. No, I mean... No, I booked myself into a junior suite at the Comfort Inn in Elmdale, thank you.'
jokes.setup: John offers Dee Dee a room at the motel
jokes.punchline: Her triple 'No, God, no' is devastating but she immediately softens it by specifying she's at the Comfort Inn — not a luxury hotel — suggesting this is still beneath her but more acceptable than this
jokes.explanation: The 'No. God, no. No' rhythm is excellent — a triple denial with escalating panic. The Comfort Inn landing is the secondary joke. Works well as character establishment for Dee Dee's snobbery-adjacent obliviousness.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait, when was the last time we actually saw each other?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, that would be your birthday party. I brought that lovely man I was seeing at the time.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You mean the one who spent the entire evening in the pool house discussing his cryptocurrency venture?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, well, he was very passionate about blockchain.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: He was naked and claiming to be a dolphin.

jokes.text: Dee Dee: 'This is very darling. It's just, it doesn't have a gym and I'm kind of in the best shape of my life right now, and I kind of wanna keep it that way.'
jokes.setup: Dee Dee is explaining why she won't stay at the motel where her sister is living in poverty
jokes.punchline: She softens the snub by calling it 'darling' but the real reason is gym access, which she delivers with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: Functionally funny — the obliviousness of prioritizing gym access when your sister is destitute. Not particularly novel but lands cleanly.

dialogue.speaker: Dee Dee
dialogue.line: Here, I want you to have this.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, a hundred dollars?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dee Dee, this is... this is too much.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No, no, it's perfect. This is literally life-changing.

jokes.text: Stevie and David going back and forth about whether Grant (the handyman) works out: 'Okay, so you do work out. You are such a liar.'
jokes.setup: David is teasing Stevie about her attraction to Grant, who claimed his physique came just from being a handyman
jokes.punchline: Stevie catches him in the lie about not working out — the banter reveals her interest in him
jokes.explanation: Light flirtatious banter comedy — functional and moves the story along, but not a particularly strong joke. The chemistry between Stevie and David around this third party is the actual entertainment.

dialogue.speaker: Dee Dee
dialogue.line: I can't stay at the motel. I do hot yoga every morning at 5 AM, and I need a space with proper ventilation and natural light. Plus, I have my evening Pilates, my afternoon spin class, and my morning run. I'm on a very strict regimen.

jokes.text: David about Grant: 'He was also just trying to tell me that he got that body from just being a handyman.' Dee Dee: 'Well, isn't Grant just a big, fat liar.'
jokes.setup: David is explaining the handyman debate to Dee Dee
jokes.punchline: Dee Dee's verdict — 'Well, isn't Grant just a big, fat liar' — is delivered with cheerful directness that's funnier for its casualness
jokes.explanation: Dee Dee's blunt verdict lands well because it matches David's sardonic register. The two characters briefly in sync is a small treat. The phrase 'big, fat liar' is charmingly excessive.

dialogue.speaker: Grant
dialogue.line: My handyman work keeps me fit.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Does it though?

jokes.text: Grant: 'I gotta grab the right Marrette, so.' — awkward exit after being caught in the workout lie
jokes.setup: Grant has been exposed as lying about his workout routine
jokes.punchline: He manufactures an excuse to flee using handyman jargon ('Marrette')
jokes.explanation: The awkward exit is a standard beat but the specific handyman detail gives it a touch of specificity. Mild laugh, functional.

dialogue.speaker: Grant
dialogue.line: I don't do anything special. It's just from doing repairs around the house.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, that's not true. Nobody gets a body like that from just doing repairs. You must be working out.

jokes.text: Dee Dee: 'Wow. He is wearing a tight shirt.'
jokes.setup: Grant has just awkwardly exited after being caught lying
jokes.punchline: Dee Dee's only comment is an appreciative assessment of Grant's physique — completely ignoring all social context
jokes.explanation: Pure economy — five words that perfectly encapsulate Dee Dee's oblivious vanity. It cuts through all the social maneuvering and gets a laugh for its bluntness. Dee Dee-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Grant
dialogue.line: I'm just gonna... I'm sensing some energy here that maybe I should not be a part of.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: There's no energy.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Zero energy.

dialogue.speaker: Grant
dialogue.line: Right, okay. Because it feels like there's... a lot of energy.

jokes.text: Dee Dee: 'So, is he gonna do some repairs on your... On your undies?' Stevie: 'Who's to say he hasn't already?'
jokes.setup: Dee Dee is asking Stevie about her interest in Grant
jokes.punchline: Stevie's deadpan response implying a prior sexual encounter
jokes.explanation: Stevie's comeback is the stronger half — her 'Who's to say he hasn't already?' is perfectly deadpan and confident. The setup from Dee Dee is a bit clunky ('undies') but Stevie's response elevates it. Good character moment for Stevie.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: He was attractive.

jokes.text: David: 'No. No. Do you. Do him. Do both. Do things. Yeah.'
jokes.setup: Stevie reveals she may have already slept with Grant and asks if that's a problem for David
jokes.punchline: David's increasingly flustered attempt to be non-possessive devolves into incoherence
jokes.explanation: This is excellent David comedy — his veneer of cool detachment crumbling in real time. 'Do things. Yeah.' is a perfect collapse of articulation. The escalating fragmentation of his speech reveals jealousy he's trying to hide. Strong character integration and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I'm sure Grant will do a very thorough job. He's very... hands-on. Very good with his... hands. And other things.

jokes.text: David: 'Let's put up some boundaries for the infrequent benefits that our friendship has.'
jokes.setup: David and Stevie are trying to establish rules for their friends-with-benefits arrangement
jokes.punchline: 'Infrequent benefits' — his qualifier immediately undersells their relationship while trying to establish emotional distance
jokes.explanation: The self-undermining qualifier 'infrequent benefits' is a nice character touch — David trying to minimize the relationship even as he discusses it. Good David voice.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's great. Grant is... he's a very... he has a face. And you two are... dating. Which is... wonderful. For you. And him. Mostly him, probably.

jokes.text: David: 'Can we do that after you screw Mr Fix-it, or would you like to do it now?'
jokes.setup: They're discussing setting up boundaries for their friends-with-benefits arrangement
jokes.punchline: David's passive-aggressive jab about Grant reveals his jealousy while pretending to be practical
jokes.explanation: 'Mr Fix-it' as a dismissive nickname for Grant while pretending to be accommodating is classic David passive-aggression. The delivery carries the moment more than the writing.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We see each other... occasionally. Very occasionally. Like, Christmas occasionally. No, less than Christmas. Like, Easter occasionally.

jokes.text: Twyla: 'I'm so sorry. I really didn't think that was gonna hit you.' Alexis: 'That's okay. Your mom did the same thing to me this morning.'
jokes.setup: Twyla accidentally hits Alexis with something
jokes.punchline: Alexis reveals Twyla's mom did the same thing to her — implying a running pattern of physical mishaps in the family
jokes.explanation: Quick and punchy. The joke implies the same thing happened twice with mother and daughter — a small comedic callback to an unseen event. Economical and lands cleanly.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, so you're dating Grant now?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's wonderful. I'm sure he'll treat you with the same respect and dignity that he treats his hemp clothing line.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I hope you two are very happy together. I'll just be here, alone, perfecting my collection of vintage brooches.

jokes.text: Twyla: 'He was a roadie with Fleetwood Mac and that was the last thing Mick Fleetwood said to him before the band hit him with a restraining order.'
jokes.setup: Twyla quotes her dad: 'When in doubt, say it with a song,' calling it wisdom
jokes.punchline: The wisdom came from Mick Fleetwood right before the band got a restraining order against the dad — recontextualizing the 'wisdom' as a warning sign of obsession
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's strongest joke up to this point and one of the best in the episode. The structure is perfectly layered: Twyla delivers 'wisdom' from her dad as genuine life advice, the Fleetwood Mac detail elevates it, and then the restraining order completely inverts the lesson — what Mick Fleetwood said was probably 'don't stalk us.' Only Twyla could deliver this with sincere warmth. The dark backstory implied (a father stalking Fleetwood Mac) is absurd and specific. Highly quotable, excellent earned comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: I'm so sorry I hit you, Alexis. It was an accident.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It's okay, Twyla.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, if it makes you feel any better, I also accidentally hit her earlier.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'I tried it once and the guy ripped the guitar out of my hands and he just started smashing it on the ground.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is advising Twyla against writing a song for Mutt
jokes.punchline: Alexis reveals she once serenaded someone and they destroyed the guitar — implying the song was that bad (or the person that volatile)
jokes.explanation: Good Alexis backstory gag. The image of someone ripping a guitar and smashing it is vivid and extreme — and the 'super angry Marine' detail that follows escalates it. Alexis's casual delivery of traumatic anecdotes is a well-established character signature.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: You know, I have a restraining order from Fleetwood Mac.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Granted, I am tone deaf and he was a... He was a super angry Marine, but...'
jokes.setup: Alexis has just described the guitar-smashing incident
jokes.punchline: She adds the mitigating detail that she's tone deaf AND the man was a 'super angry Marine' — both factors rendering the serenading a disaster
jokes.explanation: The escalation of the callback is excellent — each added detail makes the original story funnier. 'Super angry Marine' is a beautifully specific qualifier. The implied pairing (tone-deaf Alexis serenade + super angry Marine) is perfectly absurd. The 'but...' trailing off suggests even she knows this is indefensible.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: My dad always said, 'If you can't express your feelings in words, express them in song.'

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: So I wrote a song for this boy I liked in high school. I performed it in front of the whole cafeteria.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Turns out, he didn't appreciate it. He got a restraining order.

jokes.text: Jocelyn to David: 'You strike me as the sort of person that had a hard time in high school.'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is about to ask David a favor
jokes.punchline: Her opening gambit for connecting with him is pointing out that he looks like someone who suffered socially — delivered with cheerful sincerity
jokes.explanation: The line works on multiple levels: it's both an insult and an attempt at empathy from Jocelyn who doesn't realize how it lands. David's response — 'Thank you.' — is the perfect reaction, played as if receiving a compliment. Strong cringe comedy with good character work from both.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Don't write songs for people you're dating. It never ends well.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I wrote a song for my ex once, and a Marine showed up at my apartment with a machete.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Turns out it was his ex's favorite song, and he thought I was mocking him.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So now I have a strict policy: no songs, no Marines with weapons.

jokes.text: Jocelyn: 'Your floral top, for instance.' — as evidence that David had a hard time in high school
jokes.setup: Jocelyn doubles down on her read of David by citing a specific piece of evidence
jokes.punchline: His shirt is exhibit A — she doesn't elaborate, she doesn't have to
jokes.explanation: The continuation of the previous joke — Jocelyn produces her evidence and it's the shirt. The brevity of 'Your floral top, for instance.' is a perfect comedy beat. Works as a visual gag too (we can infer David's distinctive fashion). Excellent economy.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: David, I need your help with one of my students.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Of course. What's the problem?

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Well, he's very difficult to work with. Kind of like you, actually, which is why I thought you'd be perfect for this.

jokes.text: David: 'The idea of me life coaching another human being should scare you. A lot. I don't...'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn asks David to come mentor a struggling teen
jokes.punchline: David's self-aware acknowledgment that he is uniquely unqualified for this role, delivered with matter-of-fact concern
jokes.explanation: Good self-deprecating David humor. The line works because it's true — David is a terrible mentor candidate — and because he's warning Jocelyn about himself rather than just refusing. 'A lot. I don't...' trailing off is a nice touch.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David would understand being an outcast. Have you seen what he wears?

jokes.text: Jocelyn as she leaves: 'Oh, and, honey, get the quilted, that other stuff will chafe you right up.'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is exiting after convincing David to help the teen
jokes.punchline: She exits with unsolicited toilet paper advice — a warm but deeply incongruous parting shot
jokes.explanation: This is a great Jocelyn joke. The pivot from emotional conversation to practical toilet paper advice is completely incongruous and totally in character. 'Chafe you right up' is a wonderful phrase. It also implies she noticed what he was shopping for. Genuinely warm and funny — the kind of small-town practical advice the Roses keep receiving.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I appreciate the offer, but I think you've made a terrible mistake. I'm not equipped to mentor anyone. I can barely manage myself. I have crippling anxiety, I'm judgmental, I'm often dismissive, and I have absolutely no patience for incompetence or poor life choices. So unless this troubled teen is looking to learn how to be a self-involved disaster, I'm probably not your guy.

jokes.text: David: 'Yeah. The actual longest relationship was, um, a three-month affair with a Saudi prince, but for the last two months of that, I was trapped in his palace trying to get to an embassy.'
jokes.setup: Stevie has asked what 'voluntarily' means in context
jokes.punchline: David matter-of-factly describes being held captive in a Saudi prince's palace for two-thirds of a relationship
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The Saudi prince detail alone is good, but 'for the last two months of that, I was trapped in his palace trying to get to an embassy' is exceptional — the casual specificity (embassy!) implies David has experience navigating international diplomatic escape routes. The absurdity escalates with each clause. Only David Rose could deliver this as a mild clarification. Highly quotable, deeply character-rooted. Comparable to an 8+ anchor.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I appreciate the gesture, but I'm not really equipped to mentor anyone. I'm self-absorbed, I have unrealistic expectations, and I'm not particularly patient.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: That's so humble of you to say. You know, that kind of self-awareness is exactly what makes you such a great mentor.

jokes.text: Moira to Dee Dee finding her outside: 'How did you find me?' Dee Dee: 'Well, where else is there to go? And Johnny may have told me.'
jokes.setup: Moira has retreated somewhere to avoid her sister
jokes.punchline: Dee Dee's logic — 'where else is there to go?' — underscores the smallness of Schitt's Creek, then deflates with John's betrayal
jokes.explanation: Functional joke about the town's size and John's inability to keep a secret. The 'and Johnny may have told me' undercut is the better half.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Well, if you ever need help with a troubled teen, you just let me know.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Thank you, Jocelyn. That's very kind.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: And one more thing — you should really be using a bidet. It's much better for you.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Thank you, Johnny.'
jokes.setup: Dee Dee reveals John told her where Moira was hiding
jokes.punchline: Moira's pointed 'Thank you, Johnny' — aimed at an absent John — is loaded with quiet fury
jokes.explanation: Classic sitcom burn — perfectly calibrated passive aggression addressed to someone not present. Moira's delivery would elevate this considerably on screen.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: My longest relationship was actually voluntary.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, most of it was captivity, but like, the first few weeks were totally voluntary.

jokes.text: Moira: '$50,000. Does that sound familiar?' Dee Dee: 'Yes. You gave that to me as a down payment...' Moira: 'No, loaned it.'
jokes.setup: Moira wants an explanation for why Dee Dee owes them
jokes.punchline: Dee Dee's immediate reframing of the loan as a 'gift' vs. Moira's sharp correction 'No, loaned it'
jokes.explanation: The give/loan distinction is a reliable comedy bit about family money dynamics. The sharp interjection timing is good.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Fine? FINE?! Mutt, no. No, no, no. You don't describe a relationship as 'fine.' Fine is what you say when someone asks how your day was and you spent it waiting in the DMV. Fine is not a word that should ever be associated with love.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You should be saying things like 'amazing' or 'incredible' or 'I've never been happier.' Fine is the relationship equivalent of beige. And Twyla is not beige, Mutt. Twyla is, like, a really pretty jewel tone.

jokes.text: We handed you the money and you said... [long pause] / Thank you? / Ugh. Too late.
jokes.setup: Moira is trying to get Dee Dee to admit she didn't properly thank them when she took the money
jokes.punchline: The pause before Dee Dee's tentative 'Thank you?' — offered far too late and clearly not sincere — gets a disgusted 'Too late' from Moira
jokes.explanation: The comedic pause is doing the heavy lifting. Dee Dee's 'Thank you?' is a confused, belated attempt to give the right answer; Moira's 'Too late' is a perfect, clipped dismissal. The structure (setup → pause → wrong answer → dismissal) is clean and well-timed. Good cringe rhythm.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm not hiding, I'm simply... reconsidering my life choices in this closet.

jokes.text: Dee Dee: 'Well, Moira, considering you had over $500 million in the bank, I'm a little personally offended that I didn't get more.'
jokes.setup: Moira is pressing Dee Dee about repaying the $50,000 loan
jokes.punchline: Dee Dee's counter-offense: she's insulted she only got $50,000 given their total wealth — inverting the debtor's position entirely
jokes.explanation: Brilliant inversion — the person who owes money manages to take offense that they weren't given more. Only Dee Dee could pull this off sincerely. It's peak oblivious entitlement and very funny. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Dee Dee
dialogue.line: Well, there's only so many places to hide in a town this size. Plus your husband totally ratted you out.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I didn't rat her out!

dialogue.speaker: Dee Dee
dialogue.line: You literally told me you were at the market.

jokes.text: John: 'So walk me through this, she offered you a cheque and you turned it down?'
jokes.setup: Moira has rejected Dee Dee's peace offering on principle
jokes.punchline: John's incredulous but controlled summary of the situation
jokes.explanation: Standard straight-man reaction. John's disbelief is earned but the line itself isn't particularly crafted. Functional.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Did you say 'Thank you, Moira, you're a saint'?

dialogue.speaker: Dee Dee
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: John: 'Moira, the woman dates dive-bar owners, okay? Let her insult us.'
jokes.setup: Moira is offended by Dee Dee's insults and refuses the money on principle
jokes.punchline: John argues that since Dee Dee has poor taste (evidenced by dating dive-bar owners), her insults don't count and should be ignored in exchange for cash
jokes.explanation: Excellent John line. His logic is impeccably transactional — he's found a way to rationalize accepting insults by discrediting the insulter. 'Let her insult us' is a great beat because it inverts the natural instinct to defend one's dignity. Very John Rose — pragmatic, slightly desperate, but delivered with calm authority. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Dee Dee
dialogue.line: I deserved more money given your wealth!

jokes.text: John: 'You talk about bed bugs? That cheque could pay for fumigation. Better yet, we could move to a place that doesn't have bed bugs.'
jokes.setup: John is making the case for accepting Dee Dee's money
jokes.punchline: He callbacks the opening bed bug scare to argue for taking the cheque — connecting financial desperation to domestic horror
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the cold open. John using the bed bug crisis as a practical argument for accepting charity is very in-character. The line 'move to a place that doesn't have bed bugs' is quietly sad and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Dee Dee
dialogue.line: I didn't want to hurt your feelings.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, well that's very thoughtful of you.

dialogue.speaker: Dee Dee
dialogue.line: Wait, no. I immediately remembered you're heartless.

jokes.text: John: 'And you spent more than that on wigs that year.'
jokes.setup: Moira insists she wants what Dee Dee owes them, not charity
jokes.punchline: John reveals Moira spent MORE than the $50K loan on wigs in the same year — contextualizing how meaningless that money actually was to them
jokes.explanation: Exceptional line — devastating specificity. The fact that it's 'wigs' (not just 'clothes' or 'accessories') makes it deeply Moira-specific. It simultaneously roasts her extravagance and makes John's pragmatism more credible. Short, sharp, and layered. One of the episode's best lines — earns its high character integration score.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, you need to take this money. It's a good opportunity.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: But what does Dee Dee think?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, Dee Dee is dating a man who lives in a storage unit. I don't think we should be taking life advice from her.

jokes.text: Moira: 'It's all I have left, John, literally.' John: 'Swallow it, Moira.' Moira: 'I can't!' John: 'Yes, you can. And then we can buy a case of champagne to wash it down with.'
jokes.setup: Moira says her pride is 'all I have left' and she can't swallow it
jokes.punchline: John extends the metaphor — they can buy champagne to wash down the swallowed pride, which also implies they desperately need that money
jokes.explanation: The champagne extension of the swallowing metaphor is warm and clever. It's both a joke and a character beat — John is gently persuading Moira while making her laugh. The callback to their extravagant past (champagne) gives it bittersweet weight. Good construction.

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: You know, we had bed bugs when we first moved here. Do you remember that?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, of course I remember that, John. I was there.

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: Well, we didn't let anyone help us then, and we're not going to start now.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Like one of those guys that has like a candy bowl of condoms on his bedside table or something.' David: 'Oh, I hope so. Wonder what colour I'll get.'
jokes.setup: Stevie describes Grant as a 'respectable guy'
jokes.punchline: Stevie's 'candy bowl of condoms' image, followed by Stevie treating it like literal candy with 'Wonder what colour I'll get'
jokes.explanation: The 'candy bowl of condoms' image is original and memorable — it paints a very specific picture of prepared-but-fun masculinity. Stevie's follow-up treating it like a candy selection is the perfect Stevie deadpan. The whole exchange is economical and distinctly voiced by both characters.

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: Fifty thousand dollars is a lot of money.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Is it though? Because that's what I spend on wigs in a month.

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: No, it's not.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, it is for me.

jokes.text: David: 'I am mentoring a teen in crisis, later. That's what I'm doing. One of Jocelyn's students, so. He's being bullied because he's different, so I'm helping to change his life. Giving back. So, yeah.'
jokes.setup: David is deflecting from jealousy by announcing his charitable activity
jokes.punchline: The increasingly desperate self-justification — 'Giving back. So, yeah.' — reveals he's trying to win a moral point over Stevie's date
jokes.explanation: The trailing 'Giving back. So, yeah.' is the funny part — David performing virtue in a transparent bid for moral high ground. Good character comedy but a bit padded.

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: You need to swallow your pride.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: With what? Literal champagne?

jokes.text: Stevie: 'And on a scale of one to 10, how much are you looking forward to that?' David: 'Three point five.'
jokes.setup: Stevie asks David how excited he is about his mentoring session
jokes.punchline: 'Three point five' — the precision of the low score, not even a round number, is the joke
jokes.explanation: Very Schitt's Creek. The decimal point in '3.5' is the entire joke — David has calibrated his reluctance to exactly 35% enthusiasm. It's both self-aware and characteristically specific. The economy is near-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: He's very respectable. He has a drawer dedicated to condoms. It's color-coordinated by size and expiration date.

jokes.text: David to Stevie about Grant: 'I'm gonna leave you be because it's not every day you get to go on a date with a guy who owns a windowless van, so that...'
jokes.setup: David is making his exit while taking a parting shot at Grant
jokes.punchline: Describing Grant's van as 'windowless' — classic predator/serial killer signifier deployed as a casual dig
jokes.explanation: Good David snark — the 'windowless van' detail implies something sinister about Grant that we're meant to understand is just jealousy. The trailing 'so that...' adds a nice air of faux-concern. Efficient and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Wait, he has different kinds?

jokes.text: Grant: 'What're you doing? You checking out porn again?'
jokes.setup: David and Stevie are caught on Stevie's computer in an ambiguous situation
jokes.punchline: Grant's casual accusation of porn-watching as his first assumption
jokes.explanation: Low-effort shock gag. 'You checking out porn again' is a cheap laugh that doesn't tell us much about any character. The 'again' implies a history which is mildly interesting but underdeveloped.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I would say my enthusiasm is hovering somewhere around a two, maybe a two-and-a-half.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And that's only because I'm not allowed to go lower, or I would be in the negatives. And I think Grant's van is very creepy.

jokes.text: Such a weird dude. / Yes. Yes, he is.
jokes.setup: David has left after Grant's porn comment, saying 'Warmest regards. Best wishes.'
jokes.punchline: Grant and Stevie bond over David's strangeness — Stevie's 'Yes. Yes, he is.' is an affectionate but completely sincere agreement
jokes.explanation: A soft landing for the scene. The double 'Yes. Yes, he is.' implies Stevie has given this considerable thought. Warm and functional but not a strong joke on its own.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Such a weird dude.' — after David's elaborate exit
jokes.setup: David has made an increasingly formal and strange exit ('Warmest regards... Best wishes')
jokes.punchline: Stevie's flat assessment: 'Such a weird dude.'
jokes.explanation: Stevie's flat verdict on David is a reliable comedic beat. 'Such a weird dude' with Grant agreeing is a good laugh, and the economy is perfect. Doesn't overstay.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I'll just... I'm going to go. Yes. Thank you for the... the time we spent together in this room. It's been... statistically significant.

jokes.text: David's awkward pause looking at Connor before saying 'Apparently you're having trouble fitting in.'
jokes.setup: David is supposed to be mentoring Connor, a struggling teen
jokes.punchline: The long pause before David states the obvious makes clear he has no idea how to do this
jokes.explanation: The inferred awkward pause before stating the problem is classic cringe mentoring comedy. David's total unpreparedness is the joke. Functional.

dialogue.speaker: Grant
dialogue.line: That was weird.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: So weird.

jokes.text: Connor: 'Are you like a doctor, or something? A psychologist?' David: 'Am I what? Uh, no.'
jokes.setup: Connor is trying to understand David's credentials
jokes.punchline: David's affronted 'Am I what?' before deflating with 'no' — as if the suggestion that he's a professional is almost insulting
jokes.explanation: David's micro-offense at being asked if he's a doctor is funny — like the idea itself is confusing to him. Good small moment.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'd be happy to mentor you. I have a lot of experience in retail and business.

dialogue.speaker: Teen
dialogue.line: Yeah, no thanks. I'm good.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, well, I think you'd really benefit from—

dialogue.speaker: Teen
dialogue.line: No, I mean... have you looked in a mirror?

jokes.text: Connor: 'Look at you. Look at your pants.'
jokes.setup: Connor is dismissing David's authority as a mentor
jokes.punchline: His evidence that David can't help him: David's distinctive/unusual pants
jokes.explanation: Echoes the 'floral top' joke — David's fashion is repeatedly used as evidence of his outsider status. Connor's two-sentence dismissal ('Look at you. Look at your pants.') is beautifully economical and specifically targeted. The repetition of the clothing critique across the episode develops into a running gag.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You know, Connor, fitting in doesn't have to be so difficult. You just need to be yourself.

dialogue.speaker: Connor
dialogue.line: I appreciate that, David. But here's the thing — I don't actually want to fit in. I choose not to. And that's my choice to make.

jokes.text: Connor: 'I'm a 16-year-old gay kid living in a town that makes me wanna throw up. The issue isn't me not fitting in. It's me not wanting to fit in.'
jokes.setup: David tries to engage Connor about fitting in
jokes.punchline: Connor delivers a sharper, more self-aware diagnosis of his situation than David could — flipping the mentor dynamic entirely
jokes.explanation: This lands as comedy because Connor is more David than David — sharper, more self-aware, and completely unwilling to accept the narrative he's been placed in. 'A town that makes me wanna throw up' is vivid and honest. The distinction 'not fitting in vs. not wanting to fit in' is a real insight delivered as a dismissal. The joke is that the mentee is more emotionally intelligent than the mentor.

dialogue.speaker: Dee Dee
dialogue.line: I'm thinking the pork chop. Or maybe the pork belly. Oh! The pork shoulder!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, well, I'll have the salad.

dialogue.speaker: Dee Dee
dialogue.line: Don't you want to try the pork ribs? They're supposed to be incredible.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No, I'm fine with the salad. Just the salad.

jokes.text: Dee Dee at dinner: 'Well, I hear the pork is very good here. The ribs, the chops, the roast.' Moira: 'Nothing for me, thanks.'
jokes.setup: The family is attempting a reconciliation dinner
jokes.punchline: Dee Dee conspicuously and enthusiastically orders pork after John has told her Moira doesn't eat there — it's a deliberate provocation
jokes.explanation: Dee Dee weaponizing pork as a passive-aggressive move while Moira refuses to eat is a good family dynamics joke. The escalating specificity ('ribs, the chops, the roast') shows she's making a point.

dialogue.speaker: Dee Dee
dialogue.line: You're being very childish about this, Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm not accepting your money or your apology.

dialogue.speaker: Dee Dee
dialogue.line: Well, if you won't accept my apology, then I suppose I'll just have to tell everyone in town that you refused to forgive me. How will that look?

jokes.text: Go ahead and order whatever you want. This is on us. / They're letting you run up a long tab, are they?
jokes.setup: John magnanimously offers to treat Dee Dee at the restaurant
jokes.punchline: Dee Dee immediately questions whether the Roses can actually afford this — undercutting John's generosity by pointing out their financial reality
jokes.explanation: A pointed but not devastating joke. The irony of the bankrupt family insisting on treating their wealthy sister — and her knowing to question it — is a nice class reversal.

dialogue.speaker: Dee Dee
dialogue.line: I came here with only the purest of intentions.

dialogue.speaker: Dee Dee
dialogue.line: Well, mostly pure. Okay, there was some gloating involved.

jokes.text: John to Dee Dee: 'Well, isn't that funny because Moira doesn't eat here, either. But I will be eating and I just don't know whether to have the ribs or the chops, but I'm definitely ordering pork.'
jokes.setup: Moira declines to eat in the restaurant where her family is having a reconciliation dinner
jokes.punchline: John, free of Moira's food politics, doubles down on the pork — implicitly aligning with Dee Dee against Moira's stubbornness
jokes.explanation: John using the pork order to subtly signal he's over Moira's stubbornness is funny but the delivery is a bit scattered. The running pork gag is mild.

dialogue.speaker: Dee Dee
dialogue.line: Look, I came here to support you, but yes, the gloating was definitely part of my plan.

jokes.text: Moira: 'If you mention pork one more time...'
jokes.setup: Everyone has been emphasizing pork at the dinner table
jokes.punchline: Moira's threat trails off — she's reached her pork threshold
jokes.explanation: The pork running gag peaks here. Moira's incomplete threat is fine but the whole pork bit is the weakest recurring element of the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Please, I need your help.

dialogue.speaker: Dee Dee
dialogue.line: Fine, I'll do it. But let's be clear — this is about power and superiority.

jokes.text: Dee Dee: 'No, no, no. I came here in the spirit of giving and forgiving. The gloating thing was a bonus.'
jokes.setup: John says he was worried Dee Dee came to gloat
jokes.punchline: Dee Dee admits the gloating was a bonus — i.e., she definitely gloated and is fine with that
jokes.explanation: One of the best Dee Dee lines in the episode. The structure is perfect: 'spirit of giving and forgiving' sounds noble, 'The gloating thing was a bonus' punctures it entirely. The calm admission that gloating occurred and was welcome is delivered without defensiveness or shame — making it funnier. Highly quotable, excellent character integration.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We're just friends. With benefits.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: What kind of benefits?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The kind where we're friends, and we also sleep together.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: So you're dating.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, we're not dating. We're just—

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Friends with benefits. Right. Which is dating.

jokes.text: Dee Dee: 'I'm gonna do this for you, but mostly, I'm doing it for me. Why? Because you can't and I can.'
jokes.setup: Dee Dee is agreeing to help the Roses financially
jokes.punchline: Her explanation of generosity is rooted in superiority — she's helping because she enjoys being able to help when others can't
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly honest and awful. Most characters would dress up their charity; Dee Dee announces it's about power dynamics. 'Because you can't and I can' is devastating and darkly funny — it reframes generosity as dominance. Strong character work, excellent economy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, when I was your age, we didn't have the internet. We had to go to the library and look things up in an encyclopedia.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So perhaps you should listen to someone with actual life experience.

jokes.text: Well, you guys gave me 50 grand, right? / Yes, we gave you 50,000 when we had the money. / But I can't... I can't ask you for $50,000. / Whatever you think is the equivalent amount.
jokes.setup: Dee Dee is figuring out how much to write the cheque for
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'whatever you think is the equivalent amount' — after pointedly reminding Dee Dee they gave it when they had money — is a dignified attempt to not beg while still clearly begging
jokes.explanation: Moira's careful phrasing to avoid explicit begging while clearly begging is good cringe comedy. 'Whatever you think is the equivalent amount' is the kind of loaded statement only someone desperately maintaining dignity would say.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Are you stable?

dialogue.speaker: Connor
dialogue.line: Do you have any alcohol?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Do you have any alcohol?

jokes.text: Connor: 'Okay, so you're telling me your girlfriend is on a date with someone else and instead of doing something about it, you're here talking to me, a kid who practically has no respect for you.'
jokes.setup: David has disclosed his Stevie/Grant situation to the teen he's supposed to be mentoring
jokes.punchline: Connor accurately diagnoses David's situation while simultaneously roasting the mentoring relationship itself
jokes.explanation: Connor's meta-awareness that he has 'practically no respect' for his mentor and says so directly is great. The inversion is complete — the mentee is now counseling the mentor. 'A kid who practically has no respect for you' as a self-description is sharp and confident.

dialogue.speaker: Connor
dialogue.line: Could you buy me some alcohol?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You know, Connor, I have to say - the fact that you're asking an adult to break the law for you shows a level of maturity I didn't know you possessed.

jokes.text: Okay, I can do you a little better than that. / [Writes the cheque] / Oh. So unnecessary, Dee Dee, but thank you.
jokes.setup: Dee Dee is about to write a cheque for more than $50,000
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'So unnecessary, Dee Dee' — when she clearly needs the money desperately — is the pride-swallowing maneuver done imperfectly; she accepts while insisting it isn't needed
jokes.explanation: 'So unnecessary' as a response to desperately needed charity is classic Moira — maintaining the performance of wealth even while accepting poverty relief. Character-specific and warm.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Do you want to hear my song?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, I don't. I hate all music.

jokes.text: David: 'Well, first of all, you're a little bitchy. Second of all, she is not my girlfriend. She's someone that I'm occasionally having sex with because we're both bored and desperate.'
jokes.setup: Connor has accurately read David's situation
jokes.punchline: David corrects Connor's framing with a definition that's arguably worse: 'because we're both bored and desperate'
jokes.explanation: David calling a teenager 'a little bitchy' is great, but 'someone that I'm occasionally having sex with because we're both bored and desperate' is the real payoff — he's correcting 'girlfriend' with something that makes the situation sound much bleaker. The self-awareness is brutal. Very quotable David line.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Grant went to bathroom and never came back

jokes.text: Connor: 'Have you not seen the 42 films they've made about it? It never works.'
jokes.setup: David explains his friends-with-benefits situation
jokes.punchline: Connor's exhausted reference to the glut of friends-with-benefits films as empirical evidence
jokes.explanation: '42 films' is a beautiful specific exaggeration. Connor citing film precedent as relationship advice is in character — he's a sharp, media-literate teen. Good observational comedy with good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Well, he's probably just been in a terrible accident. You know, something that would prevent him from texting me. Like, his hands are gone. Both of them. Severed clean off at the wrists. Or maybe he's in a coma. A very deep coma where he can't move or speak, but somehow he's still conscious enough to feel bad about it.

jokes.text: David: 'Okay, well, movies aren't always right, all right? You'll learn that later in life.'
jokes.setup: Connor cites 42 films as evidence the friends-with-benefits arrangement will fail
jokes.punchline: David dismisses film as evidence using the condescending 'you'll learn that later in life' — from a man who is very clearly proving the films right
jokes.explanation: David asserting adult authority ('you'll learn later in life') while being obviously wrong is good character comedy. His obliviousness to being the living proof of Connor's point is the irony.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What is this? What am I looking at here?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: This is insulting. This is an insult.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm paying for dinner. That's what I'm doing.

jokes.text: David: 'You're gonna be okay, right? You're stable?' Connor: 'I have a party later. Can you buy me some beer?' David: 'Uh, no. Can you buy me some beer?'
jokes.setup: David is wrapping up the mentoring session with a perfunctory welfare check
jokes.punchline: Connor asks the mentor to buy him beer; David counters by asking the teen to buy him beer
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best moments. The structure is perfect: David's perfunctory 'are you stable?' check (minimal investment), Connor's audacious beer request, and then David's mirror response. The joke completes the inversion of the mentor/mentee relationship — both are equally needy. 'Can you buy me some beer?' from David is a perfect punchline. Highly quotable, excellent structure and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: So where's my money? I gave you fifty thousand dollars.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, and I am eternally grateful. I've written you a check.

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: A check? For how much?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Forty-seven dollars and thirty-two cents.

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: Forty-seven dollars?! Moira, I loaned you fifty thousand dollars!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I've had some unexpected expenses. There was a delightful aperitif hour at the country club, and I simply had to refresh my moisturizer collection. One simply cannot put a price on self-care.

jokes.text: Alexis to Twyla: 'Um, I would love to, but I actually hate music, so I don't think I'd be a very good judge.'
jokes.setup: Twyla asks Alexis if she wants to hear some of the song
jokes.punchline: Alexis claims she hates music — a completely ridiculous statement delivered with absolute sincerity
jokes.explanation: Alexis casually announcing she hates music as if it's a mild dietary preference is wonderfully absurd. It's a deflection masquerading as genuine information. Only Alexis would sidestep this way. The follow-up ('Are you sure that Mutt likes music?') makes it even better. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: This is... this is a very small check.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Perhaps they made a decimal point error. Yes, that must be it. They simply misplaced the decimal point.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Are you sure that Mutt likes music? 'Cause I feel like if he didn't, it could be kind of awkward.'
jokes.setup: Alexis has just claimed she hates music and is discouraging Twyla's song idea
jokes.punchline: She questions whether Mutt — Twyla's boyfriend — likes music as a potential dealbreaker, as if this is a genuine concern
jokes.explanation: The callback builds the joke — Alexis wondering if Mutt might also hate music (like her) is charmingly insane. She's projecting her own absurd music-hatred onto the relationship. Good extension of the previous beat.

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: Look, Dee Dee is... she's a lot. She's exhausting, she's overbearing, she inserts herself into every single aspect of our lives, she's completely oblivious to social cues, and frankly, she's a moron.

jokes.text: Alexis (confessional): 'In all honesty, Mutt was just a placeholder. According to my tea leaves, the guy I'm supposed to marry is black, so...'
jokes.setup: Alexis apparently broke up with (or ended things with) Mutt
jokes.punchline: Her explanation: he was a 'placeholder' — and she knows her future husband's race from tea leaves
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout lines. 'Placeholder' as a relationship status is perfectly Alexis — detached, practical, ruthless. But 'According to my tea leaves, the guy I'm supposed to marry is black, so...' is genuinely brilliant: she's read tea leaves as a marriage planning tool and extracted specific demographic data from them. The 'so...' trailing off implies she has nothing further to add; the leaves have spoken. Exceptional character integration — only Alexis. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, she's not wrong.

jokes.text: David on why he's drinking: 'some snippy teen told me that my life was a mess. And I am now a single person.'
jokes.setup: David and Stevie are drinking to cope
jokes.punchline: David lists his problems: being told off by a 16-year-old, and becoming single from a relationship he'd barely acknowledged
jokes.explanation: 'Snippy teen' as a callback to Connor is nice, and 'I am now a single person' delivered flatly about a relationship he'd been minimizing all episode is a good beat. Solid if not exceptional.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'So did you spend the night washing your clothes on Grant's abs?'
jokes.setup: David asks how Stevie's date with Grant went
jokes.punchline: Stevie's absurd image of laundry-on-abs as a euphemism/image for Grant's physique
jokes.explanation: The 'washing clothes on Grant's abs' image is original and funny — it takes the earlier 'tight shirt' joke about Grant's physique and escalates it to a new absurd image. Stevie's delivery of this to David (who is clearly jealous) makes it funnier. Good economy.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Grant went to the bathroom and never came back.' ... 'So either I was stood up halfway through my date, or Grant has some serious digestive issues.'
jokes.setup: David asks how the date with Grant went
jokes.punchline: Grant ghosted her mid-date — and Stevie offers two possible explanations with deadpan equanimity
jokes.explanation: Excellent Stevie joke. 'Grant went to the bathroom and never came back' is good — but 'or Grant has some serious digestive issues' as the charitable alternative explanation is brilliant. The two options given equal weight by Stevie's deadpan is perfect character comedy. She presents being stood up and having a gastrointestinal event as equivalently plausible. Highly quotable, strong structure.

jokes.text: David: 'And because I don't have any other friends here, I can't afford to lose you, so I think it's best if we just...' Stevie: 'Friends.' David: 'Friends. Yeah.'
jokes.setup: David is trying to articulate why they should stop the friends-with-benefits arrangement
jokes.punchline: His reason for friendship: he doesn't have any other friends. Not sentiment — inventory management.
jokes.explanation: David's reason for valuing Stevie — having no other friends — is characteristically blunt and unromantic, but weirdly touching. The economy of the back-and-forth 'Friends. Friends. Yeah.' closing is good. Warm and funny simultaneously.

jokes.text: And because I don't have any other friends here, I can't afford to lose you.
jokes.setup: David is explaining why they should stop the benefits portion of their arrangement
jokes.punchline: His stated reason for valuing the friendship is purely practical — she's the only option — which is both brutally honest and accidentally sweet
jokes.explanation: David's characteristically unsentimental expression of genuine feeling — 'I can't afford to lose you' framed as a resource scarcity problem rather than an emotional statement — is perfectly him. The honesty underneath the deflection is what makes it land.

jokes.text: John: 'And we paid for the dinner. So we lost money on the whole deal.'
jokes.setup: Dee Dee has given them a check that turns out to be smaller than expected
jokes.punchline: John's accountant-brain calculates they actually lost money on the reconciliation dinner
jokes.explanation: John's transactional framing of a family moment is very in character. The check being less than the dinner cost is an efficient comedic setup. Good John voice.

jokes.text: John: 'She missed a zero.' Moira: 'She missed a zero or two.'
jokes.setup: The Roses discover Dee Dee's check is far smaller than expected
jokes.punchline: John says she missed a zero; Moira escalates to 'or two' — both in disbelief but Moira's version implies the check might be $5 rather than $500
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'a zero' to 'or two' is well-constructed — it takes John's assessment and Moira raises it to suggest Dee Dee's check might be almost nothing. Good double act moment.

jokes.text: John: 'I'm gonna call her. I'm calling her right now, that... What a cheap-ass amount!'
jokes.setup: John and Moira are processing the tiny check from Dee Dee
jokes.punchline: The normally composed John loses it and declares the amount 'cheap-ass' — a rare John outburst
jokes.explanation: John's rare loss of composure is funny because he's normally the calm one. 'Cheap-ass amount' is an undignified phrase coming from him. Good character moment.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Your sister is a cheap-ass.' — in the dark after lights-out
jokes.setup: John has told Moira to turn off the light; they're settling into bed
jokes.punchline: Moira delivers one final verdict in the dark: 'Your sister is a cheap-ass.'
jokes.explanation: The episode closing with Moira's dark-room verdict — specifically calling her 'your sister' to place the family blame on John — is a perfect button. The economy is excellent: one sentence, lights out, done. 'Your sister' shifting ownership of Dee Dee to John in the accusation is the tiny but perfect detail. Strong closing joke.

tmdb_overview: Moira's estranged sister, Deedee, pays an unexpected visit, while David agrees to talk with one of Jocelyn's students about being "different".
insight: Character escalation hits 81 as David's anxiety spirals into absurdist territory.
